subject: Astronomy & Space
title: What Is a Supernova?
description: A tiny lesson with a big payoff: What Is a Supernova?.

hook.text: A supernova is a star's explosive death—briefly outshining an entire galaxy of billions of stars.

buildup.text: When a massive star runs out of fuel, its core collapses in seconds. The rebound blasts the outer layers into space.

discovery.text: Chinese astronomers recorded a 'guest star' in 1054 AD. The remnant is now the Crab Nebula—still expanding today.

twist.text: Supernovae forge elements heavier than iron. The gold in your ring and calcium in your bones were made in exploding stars.

climax.text: Without supernovae, the universe would contain only hydrogen and helium. Every complex element required a stellar explosion.

punchline.text: Stars die so elements can live. You are made of supernova debris.

quiz.question: Why are supernovae essential for life as we know it?
options: They create and scatter heavy elements like carbon, iron, and gold throughout space
They provide the heat needed to keep planets warm enough for life
They clear away cosmic dust that would otherwise block all starlight
